text:
The function now parses the non-RFC dialect the task asked for: `;` as the field delimiter, with quoted fields and doubled-quote escaping inside them.

How it works, reading the code: it's a single left-to-right pass with a `q` flag tracking whether the cursor is inside a quoted field. Outside quotes, a `"` opens quoting and a `;` closes the current field; inside quotes, a `;` is treated as a literal character so delimiters can be embedded. The doubled-quote case is handled by the lookahead at `line[i+1]` — when a `"` inside a quoted field is followed by another `"`, it emits a single `"` and skips the second (`i++`); a lone `"` ends the quoted section. The final field is pushed after the loop, so trailing fields and empty inputs still produce an entry.

Tracing the representative cases confirms the intended behavior: `a;b;c` → three fields, `"a;b";c` keeps the embedded semicolon as one field `a;b`, and `"a""b"` decodes to `a"b`.

A grounding note: I produced this briefing by reading and hand-tracing the source only — I did not run the file or the added cases, per the constraint on this task. So "passes the added cases" is a trace-level conclusion, not an executed test result.

Two behaviors worth flagging, both consistent with a deliberately non-RFC dialect rather than bugs:
- Quotes are honored wherever they appear, not just at the start of a field. A stray `"` mid-field opens quoting, so input like `a"b"c` collapses to `abc`. RFC parsers differ here; for this custom dialect it's internally consistent.
- An unterminated quote (`"abc` with no closing quote) is tolerated silently — the remaining characters are absorbed into the field rather than raising an error.

If you want either of those tightened (e.g. only treating `"` as special at field start, or erroring on an unbalanced quote), that's a small follow-up — say the word.
